title: Welcome to Python 🐍
instruction:
Welcome! You just picked one of the best programming languages on the planet.

Python powers Instagram, Netflix, NASA, and pretty much every AI system you have ever heard of. And the best part? It is shockingly easy to learn.

Here is your very first Python program. One line. That is it.

Hit **Run** and watch what happens.
successMessage: Did you see that?! One line of code and you just made a computer say what you told it to. That is programming. Welcome to the club.

title: Make it say your name
instruction:
Now let's make it personal.

`print()` will say whatever you put inside the quotes. It does not care if it's your name, your dog's name, or the lyrics to your favourite song.

Change `"Hello, World!"` to say hello to YOU. Put your own name in there.

Something like: `print("Hello, Alex!")`
hint: Just change the text inside the quotes. Keep the quotes — Python needs them to know it's text.

codeChecks.message: Change the message — make it say hello to you, not the world!

successMessage: Your name on a screen! The program belongs to you now. That warm fuzzy feeling? That is what programmers call 'it works'. Get used to it.

title: Say more things
instruction:
Here is something cool — you can print as many lines as you want. Each `print()` creates a new line of output.

Look at the starter code. There is a comment on line 2 starting with `#`. Comments are notes to yourself — Python completely ignores them.

Add a second `print()` below it. Tell Python something about what you are learning — like `print("I am learning Python and it is awesome.")`
hint: Add print("I am learning Python and it is awesome.") on the line below the comment.
starter:
print("Hello, Alex!")
# Add a second print() below this line

codeChecks.message: Add a second print() call below the comment.
You need two print() calls — one per line.
Replace the comment with your second print() call.

successMessage: Two lines! Python ran your code top to bottom, line by line. That is literally how all programs work — top to bottom, one instruction at a time.

title: Three things at once
instruction:
Alright, let's go bigger. Three lines.

Think of it like sending a text with three messages back to back. Python sends them in order.

Add a third `print()` below the other two. Tell Python where you are from — like `print("From London.")` or wherever you actually are.
hint: Add a third print() below the second one.
starter:
print("Hello, Alex!")
print("I am learning Python.")
# Add a third print() below

codeChecks.message: Add a third print() call — you need three total.
Replace the comment with your third print() call.

successMessage: Three lines of output. You are officially doing more than most people who 'want to learn to code someday'. You are actually doing it.

title: Comments — your secret notes
instruction:
A line starting with `#` is a **comment**. Python skips it completely — it is just a note for humans.

Good programmers write comments to explain their thinking. Not what the code does (the code shows that), but *why* they did it.

Run the code below. You will notice the comment does not show up in the output. Then change the comment to describe what this program does.
hint: Change the # comment text to something descriptive. The output will not change.
starter:
# This is a comment — Python ignores this line
print("Hello, Python!")
print("Comments are just notes for humans.")
expectedOutput: Comments are just notes for humans.
successMessage: The comment vanished! Because it was never meant for Python — it was for you. Future you will love comments when you come back to old code and think 'wait, what does this do?'

title: No main, no semicolons, no drama
instruction:
Quick comparison to show you how chill Python is.

In Java, this takes about 5 lines and some curly braces just to say hello. In C++, you need a `main()` function and a semicolon.

Python? Just write and run. No ceremony.

Run this. Then add two more `print()` lines about why Python is great. You are preaching to the choir here.
hint: Add two more print() lines below the existing ones. No semicolons, no curly braces, just vibes.
starter:
print("Python runs top to bottom.")
print("No main function needed.")
# Add two more reasons why Python is great

codeChecks.message: Add two more print() calls — four total.
Replace the comment with your two print() calls.

successMessage: Four lines, zero semicolons, zero curly braces. This is why millions of people fall in love with Python. It gets out of your way.

title: Spot the bug
instruction:
Time to play detective. This program has a bug. It will crash if you run it.

The string is not closed properly — a quote is missing.

See if you can find it and fix it before running. (Hint: look at line 1 very carefully.)
hint: The closing quote is missing on line 1. It should be: print("Python is great!")
starter:
print("Python is great!)
print("Let's fix this bug.")

codeChecks.message: Fix the missing closing quote on line 1: print("Python is great!")

successMessage: Bug squashed! That was a syntax error — one of the most common mistakes beginners make. And now you know what it looks like, you will spot it instantly next time.

title: Another sneaky bug
instruction:
Python is case-sensitive. `Print` and `print` are completely different things to Python.

`Print("Hello")` will crash. `print("Hello")` works.

This catches everyone once. Both lines below are broken. Fix them.
hint: Change Print to print (lowercase p) on both lines.
starter:
Print("Hello from Python!")
Print("Case matters here.")

codeChecks.message: Change Print to print — lowercase p. Python is case-sensitive!

successMessage: print not Print. Always lowercase. After writing a few hundred print() calls, your fingers will do this automatically. Promise.

title: Write it from scratch
instruction:
No starter code this time. The editor is empty. You are writing the whole thing.

Print exactly these three lines:
```
Python is fun.
I wrote this myself.
Chapter 1 almost done.
```

Three `print()` calls, three lines of output. You have got this.
hint: Three print() calls, one per line. Match the text exactly.

codeChecks.message: Use print() to output each line.

successMessage: You wrote that from nothing. Blank screen to working program. That is the whole skill — that is what programmers do every day.

title: Chapter 1 complete!
instruction:
Last one. Let's end this chapter with a bang.

Change the message below to say `"Chapter 1 complete!"` — because it is.
hint: Change the text inside the quotes.

codeChecks.message: Change the message to: Chapter 1 complete!

successMessage:
Chapter 1 complete!

You know print(), comments, syntax, and how to hunt bugs. In Chapter 2, we are going to start *storing* information — and that is when things get really interesting.
